tools.description:
List all files and directories recursively in the Jupyter server's file system.
Used to explore the file system structure of the Jupyter server or to find specific files or directories.

limit.description: Maximum number of items to return (0 means no limit)
limit.title: Limit

max_depth.description: Maximum depth to recurse into subdirectories
max_depth.title: Max Depth

path.description: The starting path to list from (empty string means root directory)
path.title: Path

pattern.description: Glob pattern to filter file paths
pattern.title: Pattern

tools.name: list_files

result.description: Tab-separated table with columns: Path, Type, Size, Last_Modified. Includes pagination info header.
result.title: Result

tools.description:
List all available kernels in the Jupyter server.

This tool shows all running and available kernel sessions on the Jupyter server,
including their IDs, names, states, connection information, and kernel specifications.
Useful for monitoring kernel resources and identifying specific kernels for connection.
tools.name: list_kernels

result.description: Tab-separated table with columns: ID, Name, Display_Name, Language, State, Connections, Last_Activity, Environment
result.title: Result

tools.description:
Use a notebook and activate it for following cell operations.
All cell operations will be performed on the currently activated notebook.
Activate new notebook will deactivate the previously activated notebook.
Reactivate previously activated notebook using same notebook_name and notebook_path.

kernel_id.description: Specific kernel ID to use (will create new if skipped)
kernel_id.title: Kernel Id

mode.description: Notebook operation mode: 'connect' to connect to existing and activate it, 'create' to create new and activate it
mode.title: Mode

notebook_path.description: Path to the notebook file, relative to the Jupyter server root (e.g. 'notebook.ipynb')
notebook_path.title: Notebook Path

tools.name: use_notebook

tools.description: List all notebooks that have been used via use_notebook tool
tools.name: list_notebooks

result.description: TSV formatted table with notebook information
result.title: Result

tools.description: Restart the kernel for a specific notebook.
tools.name: restart_notebook

tools.description: Unuse from a specific notebook and release its resources.
tools.name: unuse_notebook

tools.description:
Read a notebook and return index, source content, type, execution count of each cell.

Using brief format to get a quick overview of the notebook structure and it's useful for locating specific cells for operations like delete or insert.
Using detailed format to get detailed information of the notebook and it's useful for debugging and analysis.

It is recommended to use brief format with larger limit to get a overview of the notebook structure, 
then use detailed format with exact index and limit to get the detailed information of some specific cells.

limit.description: Maximum number of items to return (0 means no limit)
limit.title: Limit

response_format.description: Response format: 'brief' will return first line and lines number, 'detailed' will return full cell source
response_format.title: Response Format

tools.name: read_notebook

result.description: Notebook content in the requested format
result.title: Result

tools.description: Insert a cell to specified position from the currently activated notebook.

cell_index.description: Target index for insertion (0-based), use -1 to append at end
cell_index.title: Cell Index

tools.name: insert_cell

result.description: Success message and the structure of its surrounding cells
result.title: Result

tools.description:
Replace the entire source of a cell in the currently activated notebook.
Returns a diff showing the changes made.

Use this when rewriting a cell completely. For small, targeted changes,
prefer edit_cell_source instead — it is safer for partial edits.

cell_index.description: Index of the cell to overwrite (0-based)
cell_index.title: Cell Index

tools.name: overwrite_cell_source

result.description: Success message with diff showing changes made
result.title: Result

tools.description:
Perform a surgical find-and-replace within a cell's source (like an editor's Edit tool).
Finds `old_string` in the cell and replaces it with `new_string`. Matching is literal
(not regex) and may span multiple lines. By default, `old_string` must appear exactly once;
set `replace_all=True` for multiple occurrences. Returns a diff of the changes made.

Prefer this over overwrite_cell_source for small, targeted edits — it is safer because
unchanged parts of the cell are left untouched. Use read_cell first to see the current
source and construct an accurate old_string.

cell_index.description: Index of the cell to edit (0-based)
cell_index.title: Cell Index

old_string.description: Exact string to find in cell source
old_string.title: Old String

tools.name: edit_cell_source

result.description: Success message with diff showing changes made
result.title: Result

tools.description: Execute a cell from the currently activated notebook with timeout and return it's outputs

cell_index.description: Index of the cell to execute (0-based)
cell_index.title: Cell Index

progress_interval.description: Seconds between progress updates when stream=True
progress_interval.title: Progress Interval

stream.description: Enable streaming progress (including time indicator) updates for long-running cells
stream.title: Stream

timeout.description: Maximum seconds to wait for execution
timeout.title: Timeout

tools.name: execute_cell

tools.description:
Insert a cell at specified index from the currently activated notebook and then execute it with timeout and return it's outputs
It is a shortcut tool for insert_cell and execute_cell tools, recommended to use if you want to insert a cell and execute it at the same time

cell_index.description: Index of the cell to insert and execute (0-based)
cell_index.title: Cell Index

timeout.description: Maximum seconds to wait for execution
timeout.title: Timeout

tools.name: insert_execute_code_cell

tools.description: Read a specific cell from the currently activated notebook and return it's metadata (index, type, execution count), source and outputs (for code cells)

cell_index.description: Index of the cell to read (0-based)
cell_index.title: Cell Index

include_outputs.description: Include outputs in the response (only for code cells)
include_outputs.title: Include Outputs

tools.name: read_cell

tools.description: Delete specific cells from the currently activated notebook and return the cell source of deleted cells (if include_source=True).

cell_indices.description: List of cell indices to delete (0-based)
cell_indices.title: Cell Indices

include_source.description: Whether to include the source of deleted cells
include_source.title: Include Source

tools.name: delete_cell

result.description: Success message with list of deleted cells and their source (if include_source=True)
result.title: Result

tools.description:
Move a cell from source_index to target_index within the currently activated notebook.

The cell is removed from source_index and placed at target_index. Cells in between shift
to fill the gap. The cell's type, source, and outputs are preserved.
Example: in a notebook [A, B, C, D], move_cell(1, 3) produces [A, C, D, B].

Use this tool instead of manually deleting and re-inserting a cell — it is atomic and
preserves cell metadata. Use read_notebook first to see cell indices if needed.

source_index.description: Index of the cell to move (0-based)
source_index.title: Source Index

target_index.description: Destination index where the cell will end up (0-based)
target_index.title: Target Index

tools.name: move_cell

result.description: Success message with moved cell info and surrounding context
result.title: Result

tools.description:
Execute code directly in a kernel (not saved to notebook).

Targets the current activated notebook's kernel by default. Pass kernel_id
to execute in a specific kernel directly — including raw kernels with no
notebook attached.

Recommended to use in following cases:
1. Execute Jupyter magic commands(e.g., `%timeit`, `%pip install xxx`)
2. Performance profiling and debugging.
3. View intermediate variable values(e.g., `print(xxx)`, `df.head()`)
4. Temporary calculations and quick tests(e.g., `np.mean(df['xxx'])`)
5. Execute Shell commands in Jupyter server(e.g., `!git xxx`)

Under no circumstances should you use this tool to:
1. Import new modules or perform variable assignments that affect subsequent Notebook execution
2. Execute dangerous code that may harm the Jupyter server or the user's data without permission

code.description: Code to execute (supports magic commands with %, shell commands with !)
code.title: Code

kernel_id.description: Target an existing kernel by ID (e.g. a raw kernel with no notebook). If omitted, uses the current notebook's kernel.
kernel_id.title: Kernel Id

tools.name: execute_code

tools.description:
Connect to a Jupyter server dynamically with URL and token.

This tool allows you to connect to different Jupyter servers without needing to 
restart the MCP server or modify configuration files. Particularly useful when:
- Working with multiple Jupyter servers with different ports/tokens
- Jupyter server token changes dynamically
- Need to switch between different Jupyter instances

Example usage:
- "Connect to http://localhost:8888 with token abc123"
- "Connect to http://localhost:8889 without authentication"

jupyter_url.description: Jupyter server URL to connect to (e.g., 'http://localhost:8888')
jupyter_url.title: Jupyter Url

tools.name: connect_to_jupyter
